common_name: Benary's Giant Zinnia (AAS-Winning Large-Flowered Cut-Flower Zinnia Series)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier for earlier blooms; zinnias transplant poorly when older — start indoors only 3–4 weeks ahead and use peat pots to minimize root disturbance. In zone 9, direct sow March–May or start indoors February–March; a second sowing in August produces excellent fall blooms. Benary's Giant grows best in full sun with good air circulation — powdery mildew can become a problem in humid conditions with poor air movement.

directions: Sow seeds 0.25 inch deep after last frost; thin or transplant to 12–18 inches. Deadhead spent blooms to encourage continued flowering, or harvest before fully open for longest vase life. Benary's Giant is a premier cut-flower zinnia series from Ernst Benary GmbH of Germany — a series of tall, large-flowered zinnias producing the biggest blooms in the Zinnia elegans species, with individual flowers reaching 4–5 inches in diameter on sturdy stems 18–24 inches long suitable for floral arrangements. The series is available in a wide range of colors including coral, rose, scarlet, salmon, deep red, orange, gold, white, lime green, and blends; each flower is a fully double, densely petaled dahlia-like form with exceptional substance and long vase life (5–10 days). Benary's Giant received AAS recognition (2003) in multiple colors for the combination of large flower size, stem length, disease resistance relative to other tall zinnia varieties, and overall performance in North American garden trials. The series is the industry standard for professional cut-flower production of zinnias, used extensively by market gardeners and florists who want the largest, most impressive zinnia blooms for fresh-cut use. Plants produce heavily throughout summer and into fall with regular harvesting or deadheading.
sources: All-America Selections — Benary's Giant zinnia AAS winner 2003
Ernst Benary GmbH — Benary's Giant series introduction
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Cut-flower zinnia variety comparison

common_name: Profusion Zinnia (AAS-Winning Interspecific Disease-Resistant Compact Zinnia)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier; Profusion zinnias are more compact than standard Z. elegans types and well-suited to containers and border edging. In zone 9, plant March–May for summer bloom and again August for fall. Profusion's exceptional disease resistance makes it the best zinnia for humid Gulf South summers where powdery mildew devastates standard zinnia types. The plants self-clean (no deadheading needed) and bloom continuously from planting until frost.

directions: Sow seeds 0.25 inch deep after last frost; thin to 10–14 inch spacing. No deadheading required — old blooms fall cleanly and new buds replace them continuously. Profusion is a series of interspecific hybrid zinnias (Zinnia elegans × Zinnia angustifolia) developed by PanAmerican Seed and introduced in 1999, when the original Profusion Orange and Profusion Cherry received the first AAS Award ever given to a zinnia — an unprecedented recognition that launched the interspecific zinnia category. The Profusion hybrid combines the larger flower size of Z. elegans with the heat tolerance, powdery mildew resistance, and continuous-blooming, self-cleaning nature of Z. angustifolia, solving the primary problems that limit zinnia use in warm, humid climates: powdery mildew (which can defoliate standard zinnias by midsummer in the Gulf South) and the need for constant deadheading to maintain bloom. Profusion plants are compact (10–16 inches tall), dome-shaped, and covered with 2–2.5 inch single and semi-double flowers that fall cleanly when spent without intervention. The extreme heat tolerance and low maintenance make Profusion the zinnia of choice for Gulf South landscapes where most annual flowers struggle in the summer heat.
sources: All-America Selections — Profusion zinnia AAS winner 1999
PanAmerican Seed — Profusion zinnia series introduction
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flower Production for the Gulf South

common_name: Zahara Zinnia (AAS-Winning Heat-Tolerant Mildew-Resistant Landscape Zinnia)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier; Zahara is slightly larger than Profusion zinnia (15–20 inch plants vs. 10–16 inch) with bigger individual flowers. In zone 9, plant March–May for summer bloom. Zahara's exceptional powdery mildew resistance and heat tolerance make it among the most reliable summer annual flowers for the Gulf South, where it blooms continuously without disease intervention.

directions: Sow seeds 0.25 inch deep after last frost; no deadheading required. Zahara is another interspecific hybrid zinnia (Zinnia marylandica) from Ball Horticultural, closely related to the Profusion series — a compact to medium landscape zinnia with improved flower size over Profusion (2.5–3 inch flowers vs. 2–2.5 inch) while maintaining the same extraordinary disease resistance and self-cleaning, continuously-blooming habit. Zahara received AAS Awards in multiple colors including Zahara Coral Rose (2010), Zahara Sunburst (2010), Zahara White (2006), and others — a testament to the quality of the series across color ranges. The larger individual flowers compared to Profusion give Zahara a more showy appearance suitable for both landscape mass plantings and mixed containers. Like Profusion, Zahara virtually eliminates the maintenance burden of deadheading and disease management that standard zinnia types require in the Gulf South. The plants are bushy, well-branched, and mound-shaped, producing dense coverage that discourages weeds in landscape beds. Colors include fire, scarlet, coral, rose, white, yellow, orange, and bicolors.
sources: All-America Selections — Zahara zinnia multiple AAS winners
Ball Horticultural — Zahara zinnia series introduction
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Annual Flower Variety Trials

common_name: Gloriosa Daisy (AAS-Winning Large-Flowered Black-Eyed Susan Annual)

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost (seeds tolerate light frost) or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier for earlier blooms; Gloriosa Daisy is a short-lived perennial in zones 3–10 that blooms its first summer from seed. In zone 9, direct sow January–March for late spring blooms, or again in August–September for fall and winter blooms. Gloriosa Daisy self-seeds prolifically in favorable conditions, naturalizing in beds and meadows.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface (needs light for germination) 2–4 weeks before last frost; thin to 12–18 inches. Gloriosa Daisy is a cultivated selection of the native black-eyed susan (Rudbeckia hirta) that earned the first AAS Award given to a rudbeckia in 1957 — a selection that dramatically increased the flower size of the native species while maintaining the same robust, adaptable, heat-tolerant character. Standard R. hirta produces 2–3 inch flowers; Gloriosa Daisy produces flowers 4–6 inches in diameter, with the classic yellow daisy form (golden-yellow ray petals surrounding a dark brown, slightly cone-shaped central disk) scaled up to a presence visible from a distance. The flowers are sometimes semi-double and occur in solid yellow, bicolor yellow with a mahogany band at the center, solid orange, and mahogany-red forms, giving the planting a naturalistic wildflower quality. Gloriosa Daisy is a native-plant-adjacent choice for naturalistic gardens, meadow plantings, and xeric landscapes where it provides summer color with minimal supplemental irrigation. The plants attract bees, butterflies, and in fall the seed heads provide food for goldfinches and other birds. Self-seeding in place, Gloriosa Daisy can naturalize in a garden with benign neglect.
sources: All-America Selections — Gloriosa Daisy AAS winner 1957
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Rudbeckia hirta plant guide
Missouri Botanical Garden — Gloriosa Daisy cultivation notes

common_name: Indian Summer Rudbeckia (AAS 1995 Gigantic-Flowered Black-Eyed Susan Annual)

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier; Indian Summer's massive flowers need the full growing season to develop. In zone 9, direct sow January–March for spring and summer blooms. Indian Summer produces flowers over an extended season (June through frost in most zones) and the large seed heads left in place provide significant bird food in fall and winter while also self-seeding for subsequent seasons.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface (needs light) 2–4 weeks before last frost; thin to 18–24 inches for full plant development. Indian Summer is a 1995 All-America Selections-winning Rudbeckia hirta variety notable for producing what are arguably the largest flowers of any annual rudbeckia: individual blooms reach 6–9 inches in diameter — roughly the size of a dinner plate — on strong, upright stems 24–36 inches long suitable for cutting. The semi-double to fully double flowers are golden yellow with a large, prominent dark brown central cone that gives the classic black-eyed susan character at an outsized scale. The gigantic flower size is the defining trait and the primary reason for its AAS recognition — at 6–9 inches, Indian Summer flowers are 2–3 times the diameter of standard rudbeckia flowers and have a visual impact that stops gardeners in their tracks. Like all R. hirta selections, Indian Summer is extremely heat-tolerant, drought-tolerant once established, and adapted to poor, dry soils — the native habitat conditions of the prairie and meadow ecosystems from which the species originates. Plants bloom heavily from early summer through hard frost, and the large seed heads left to dry attract finches and sparrows.
sources: All-America Selections — Indian Summer rudbeckia AAS winner 1995
Burpee Seeds — Indian Summer rudbeckia variety notes
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Annual and Perennial Rudbeckia Evaluation
